result:
I.  
The first thing I notice when I wake is the sound of the neighbor’s sprinkler: a small, determined hiss that moves in an arc across the yard, returns, and begins again, as if the grass had asked to be remembered drop by drop. It is 4:47 a.m. by the stove clock, the only lit rectangle in the house. I lie still, testing the day the way a diver tests water with a toe. The bones in my ankles click. The cat, who has learned to imitate my insomnia, stands on my chest in the approximate shape of a question mark. I do not know what she is asking. Perhaps she only wants confirmation that the world is still being held together by familiar laws: gravity, habit, the fact that I will eventually stand and pour kibble into her blue bowl. I tell her yes, these things remain, and she seems satisfied, stepping down toward the foot of the bed with small diplomatic paws.

Outside, the sprinkler continues its patient argument with dawn. I think of how many mornings I have begun like this, eavesdropping on other people’s infrastructure. The neighbor—a retired surveyor named Rodriguez—once told me he sets the timer for 4:30 because that is when city pressure peaks and the cost per gallon drops a fraction of a cent. He likes the efficiency, but also the secrecy: only owls and insomniacs witness his thrift. I admire the pact he has made with the invisible, the way he trusts the municipal pipes to keep their promise. In a world that daily advertises its fractures, such an agreement feels almost tender.

II.  
By 5:10 I am at the kitchen table with a notebook open to a blank page. The cat has finished her kibble and now watches the window for the first bird foolish enough to land. I have decided to write 2,500 words about “whatever I want,” a freedom that arrives wearing the mask of anxiety. Limitless choice is a kind of vertigo; I feel the way I did at sixteen when a friend’s older brother drove us to the edge of the quarry and said, “Jump if you want, or don’t.” The water below was black, depth unknowable. Some of us jumped. Others invented reasons to retreat: stitches still healing, cramp in the calf, philosophical objection to cliffs. I stood on the lip so long the moment hardened into anecdote. Later, I told the story as if hesitancy were a clever joke rather than paralysis. Now the blank page offers the same cliff in negative space; the jump is words, the water is time, and the only witness is a cat who would sell me for a second breakfast.

III.  
I begin with the sprinkler because it is already happening, because describing the world is easier than inventing it. The phenomenologists believed consciousness is directional, always “about” something. They called this intentionality, a word that sounds like a moral compliment but is only an observation: the mind aims. Mine aims at the hiss, at the arc, at Mr. Rodriguez’s thrift. Then it aims at the memory of my father watering the lawn at dusk, dragging a green hose that leaked in three places. He wore cutoff jeans and Army boots, a combination that made him look like a refugee from his own life. The hose produced small rainbows if the angle was right; I believed they were rare, the way children believe anything that appears only once is precious. I asked him why the rainbow vanished when I tried to put my finger in it. He said, “Because some things are only meant to be looked at.” At the time I thought he meant physics. Now I think he meant marriage, war, himself.

IV.  
Word count: 417. I check because I am afraid of running out of material before I run out of mandated space. This fear is ancient; it dates back to the first time I was paid by the column-inch for a newspaper story about a neighborhood association dispute over fence heights. I interviewed a man who had erected an eight-foot cedar barricade because the neighbor’s toddler “looked at me funny.” The toddler, he claimed, was a spy for the other side. I needed 600 words. By 400 I had exhausted every quote, every ordinance, every metaphor about cedar. I began inventing adjectives: “brooding fence,”“conspiratorial pickets.” The copy editor laughed, but the check cleared, and I learned that language can be stretched like taffy if you heat it with panic.

V.  
The sky outside the kitchen window lightens to the color of a dove’s belly. A single bird lands on the bird feeder—female cardinal, head feathers ruffled as if she has just woken from an argument. The cat chatters, teeth clicking like knitting needles. I wonder what the bird thinks of the hiss next door. Does it register as rain, threat, irrelevant? The ethologists say birds categorize the world into edible, dangerous, or worth singing to. Everything else is transparent. I envy that triage. My own categories metastasize: edible, dangerous, overdue, sentimental, tax-deductible, potentially symbolic, possibly cancerous, memory of 1997, reminder to floss. The mind is a closet thrown open by a burglar in a hurry.

VI.  
I decide to write about writing, the way prisoners scratch days into plaster. The metaphor is melodramatic; I have never been imprisoned unless you count seventh-grade algebra. Still, the page keeps time. I notice that each sentence is a small negotiation with silence. Silence pays better, people say, but it refuses to bargain. Words, on the other hand, are desperate salesmen, willing to throw in adverbs if you take two metaphors. I oblige. I write: “The morning bruises into blue.” I immediately cross it out because bruises are cliché, because the morning is not blue but the color of wet newspaper. Still, the phrase lingers like a bad song, and I understand why poets speak of lines “coming” to them; words have legs, and some are burglars.

VII.  
Word count: 732. I allow myself a sip of coffee that has cooled to the temperature of regret. I remember a therapist once asking what regret tasted like. I said, “Like pennies and burnt toast.” She wrote that down, nodding as if I had paid for the session in rare coins. I never returned; I was afraid she would assign homework involving toast. Now I think regret is more like coffee abandoned: still coffee, still caffeine, but stripped of the story you rehearsed while it steamed. You return to find it waiting, honest, unbeautiful, drinkable if you are brave enough to microwave the past.

VIII.  
Mr. Rodriguez’s sprinkler shuts off at 5:45 precisely. The cessation is abrupt, like a conversation ended by slammed door. In the hush I hear the cardinal again, this time singing. Birds do not sing for joy, scientists say; they declare territory, attract mates, warn off rivals. But the distinction between warning and joy is human. We invented the fine print. The cardinal sings, and I take it personally, the way parishioners believe sunlight through stained glass is addressed to them. I write: “Song is a fence made of sound.” I leave it; even salesmen deserve a home.

IX.  
I wander into the living room where books lean against one another like travelers on a bus. I pull out a volume at random: a field guide to moths. Opening it releases a confetti of old boarding passes. I read that the Spanish moon moth has tails that spin as it flies, creating the illusion of a second head to confuse predators. I love this detail because it is both true and metaphorical. We all grow false heads: social media profiles, CVs, the cheerful answer to “How are you?” I once tried spinning my own second head at a party, claiming I was a pastry chef who specialized in funerals. People nodded politely, asked for my card. I almost apologized, but the false head had no mouth.

X.  
Word count: 1,063. My hand cramps. I switch to the laptop, whose screen glows like a hole punched in the dark. The cat claims the warm seat I vacated. She already knows the best thing about writing: it generates heat. I think of all the writers who have frozen in garrets, literal or figurative. I picture them hunched over, breath visible, inventing summer scenes to fool themselves into thaw. Maybe that is what literature is: a collective lie about temperature. We read descriptions of beaches and believe we are less cold. We read about despair and believe we are less alone, which is the same lie rotated ninety degrees.

XI.  
I open a new tab. The browser offers headlines: markets, wars, a celebrity who has said something unforgivable about whales. I close it. The mind is a hotel lobby; headlines are the tourists who claim chairs without ordering coffee. I return to the moth book. It tells me that some moths can hear sonar clicks of bats and respond by folding wings and dropping like stones. Evolution has given them an early-warning system built of nerve and scale. I wonder what human equivalent would look like: a sudden ability to duck when ideology swoops, a plummet that saves us from being devoured by certainty. I write: “Fold wings, drop, survive.” It sounds like a self-help seminar taught by insects. I would enroll.

XII.  
The sun is properly up now, light landing on the fridge magnets like a police interrogation. One magnet reads “World’s Okayest Dad.” I bought it myself, a preemptive strike against excellence. My daughter laughed when she saw it, then asked if “okayest” was a real word. I told her it was now, that language is democratic, that usage votes. She considered this, then began calling her peanut-butter sandwich “president” because she used it every day. Children are ruthless lexicographers.

XIII.  
I decide to write about her, but obliquely, the way you approach a skittish animal. She is ten, has my late mother’s widow’s peak, believes every problem can be solved by tape. Once she taped a banana to the wall and titled it “Breakfast That Never Decides.” I photographed it and texted her teacher, who replied with a emoji of applause. I worry she will grow up thinking art is easy, that Duchamp already lowered the bar and she can limbo beneath. But maybe that is the gift: to start low and build upward, the opposite of my own trajectory, which began with grandeur and is currently squatting at 1,389 words about sprinklers and moths.

XIV.  
She is still asleep, arms flung in the abandonment pose unique to children and the intoxicated. I check her room each morning the way a pilgrim checks a relic: touch the pulse, verify the miracle. She used to wake at 5:00; now she sleeps like a teenager practicing for exile. I miss the toddler who greeted me with questions: “Why is the moon sticky?” “Where do clocks go when they die?” I answered earnestly, inventing cosmologies on the spot. Now she Googles. Knowledge arrives faster but thinner, like wallpaper instead of plaster. Still, she sometimes asks me to define words she already knows, testing whether my definitions match the internet’s authority. I try to smuggle wonder into the answer: “Gravity is the way heavy things say sorry for being heavy.” She rolls her eyes, but I catch her repeating it to the cat.

XV.  
Word count: 1,574. I stretch and notice the neighbor’s sprinkler has started again. A second cycle, unscheduled. Perhaps Mr. Rodriguez is awake, tweaking dials, seeking an even more perfect fraction of a cent. Or perhaps the timer is broken, the machine gone rogue, watering long after the grass has submitted. I imagine the yard drowning, earthworms surfacing like tiny submariners abandoning ship. Excess is easier to picture than sufficiency. That is why tragedy makes better art than satisfaction; it is visual, measurable. Satisfaction is a closed circle, tragedy an arrow. Arrows travel.

XVI.  
I return to the memory of the quarry. I never jumped. Instead I walked back through the woods, inventing a victorious version in which I dove, emerged heroically, impressed everyone. By the time we reached the 7-Eleven for Slurpees, I half-believed the lie. I discover, at 42, that I am still standing at the lip, still rehearsing the leap. The blank page is my compensation: here I can dive upward, a reverse fall, and no one counts the splash. I write: “The quarry is now inside me, filled with every unjumped minute.” I read it aloud. The cat flicks an ear, unimpressed by quarries or metaphor.

XVII.  
I want to write about my father again, but the hose memory feels used up, like a cassette played too often. Instead I remember his collection of broken watches. After he died, I found dozens in a shoebox, each frozen at a different hour. Some had no hands, only gears. I asked my mother why he kept them. She said he believed time could be repaired if you found the right donor part. He never did, but he enjoyed the hunt. I think of him at the kitchen table, tweezer in hand, the overhead bulb making a moon of his bald spot. He was gentle with the tiny wheels, as if they were bird bones. Maybe this was love: not the fixing, but the willingness to keep looking. I inherited the box but not the patience. I wear a digital watch that corrects itself via satellite, a small god strapped to wrist. It is never wrong, and therefore never interesting.

XVIII.  
Word count: 1,901. I feel the way marathoners describe mile 20, the invisible wall. Words thicken, resist. I type nonsense to keep momentum: “Moth president sprinkler quarry time cat banana regret.” It looks like a ransom note written by a surrealist. I delete. I remember reading that Kerouac taped together sheets to avoid interrupting flow; I have taped together clichés. The trick is to cut between them, find the living film. I try: “The sprinkler arcs like a sentence that refuses to end, pausing only to breathe at the comma of sidewalk.” Better, but it smells of workshop. I leave it; workshops are just organized envy.

XIX.  
I open the front door to air out the staleness of effort. The yard smells of cut grass and the neighbor’s excess water. A paperboy—though there are no more boys, only adults in cars—throws a plastic bag that lands with a soft thud. Headlines have become physical again, a weighted object. I retrieve the paper and read the first line: “City Council Debates Fate of Century-Old Tree.” The tree is a sycamore, supposedly diseased, supposedly a hazard. Protesters have chained themselves to its trunk. I picture them aging in place, graying into bark, becoming part of what they defend. I want to join them, but I have 400 words left to write.

XX.  
I return to the table and write the tree: “It has stood since before the houses, its roots sipping from creeks now buried in pipes. Each spring it drops bark like a snake tired of being historical. Children have carved initials into its skin, the way prisoners mark days, believing love lasts longer than cambium.” I calculate: 2,089. I am close. The end becomes visible, a lighthouse that might be on either shore—rescue or doom, but at least a finish.

XXI.  
I decide to end with my daughter waking. I hear her footsteps now, soft thuds above. She will descend soon, hair wild, asking for pancakes shaped like galaxies. I will pour batter in spirals, and she will pronounce them inaccurate, request extra chocolate chips to represent dark matter. I will oblige, because parenting is part astronomy, part treaty. She will tell me her dream: a school bus that could only drive backward, a teacher who spoke fluent dolphin. I will listen the way I listen to the sprinkler, the cardinal, the moth: as if the world were a sentence I am still learning to read.

XXII.  
Word count: 2,314. I feel the way a carpenter might, smoothing the last edge, smelling sawdust, reluctant to hammer the final nail because then the table must bear weight. I scroll back through the mess: sprinklers, quarries, moths, watches, trees. A garage sale of imagery. But somewhere in the pile is the toy I wanted most: the moment when attention becomes affection, when looking long enough turns into love. I stop counting commas. I let the cursor blink.

XXIII.  
She appears in the doorway, clutching a stuffed narwhal. “Did you write the thing?” she asks. I nod. “Read me the best line.” I scan, find: “We all grow false heads, but song is a fence that keeps the real one safe.” She considers, then says, “It’s okay, but it needs more narwhal.” I laugh, promise revision. She pads to the couch, still half dream. I type: “Add narwhal,” save document. The cat follows, claiming morning light like a monarch. Outside, the grass drinks the last of Mr. Rodriguez’s gift. The cardinal sings again, same song, same warning, same joy. I close the laptop. The quarry inside me quiets, not filled but momentarily reflected. I have dived upward, landed here. For now, it is enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
